entry.title: Re: How to generate a wrapper for function modules (BAPIs) in tier 2
entry.content:
Hi @Andre_Fischer, It was fantastic information. We will start using this tool going forward. Thanks so much. I have tried this tool using Function Module SATC_CI_GET_RESULT which actually failed with Syntax Errors. I corrected them manually but just want to bring to your notice so that the bug can be fixed.  There are two export parameters with type "i " which are not actually defined in the Interface by the tool but referenced in the Class. I changed it from ZIF_WRAP_SATC~i to just "i" and it worked fine. 

Let me know if you need more information.

entry.title: Create new report/period in SAP Disclosure Management (manually or using roll foward)
entry.content:
I have to build a report (datacache/word) for the Group and 50 other companies in the group. The structures of both are very similar (datacache/word), apart from some notes and the data that the datacache must present from each one.
I thought of two alternatives:
1- create the main one (group - datacache/word) and then copy both to new ones (datacache/word) that would use each company (unit), which would help with the profile, but I would have to manually replace each datalink in the word chapter (200 tables each one 50 company) because they would have a link to a different datacache.
2- use the roll forward function and create a specific period for each company (2024_EMP100, 2024_EMP200, etc.), so there would be no need to replace all the datalinks in the word chapters, as the roll forward would do this.
Please, what would be the most appropriate/recommended thing to do, or would I have another option?

Thanks,

Felipe

SAP Disclosure Management @olga_vasilieva @Marc_Kuipers

entry.title: Re: GRC Master User id Mapping
entry.content:
Hi Plaban

You can use Master IDs in general for ARA for ABAP connectors too. The point is how you populate the GRACUSERMAP table. For SFEC the table is populated automatically based as part of repo synch if you user parameter 1055. As you can check in the parameter guide below the parameter is intended for SuccessFacotors Connector:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ACCESS_CONTROL/6c75bb80954e4543b4a4dfb68d6a4998/fe8f5e0ad44c4ca2a19cd8e1a3669159.html

"This parameter allows you to specify the connectors for Success Factors for which the field mapping for the field SAP_USER_ID will be considered in the Repository synchronization. The Field Mapping must be in place for the Field SAP_USER_ID for the connector group for Success Factors connector, only then it will work along with this parameter."

entry.title: Overview Page Card Navigation overwritten with Metadata Extension - Problem
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

in F2828 - Maintenance Planning Overview I changed the navigation of the card overdue Orders via Metadata Extension like this in Eclipse ADT:

 

@Metadata.layer: #CUSTOMER
annotate view C_OverdueMaintenanceOrderQ with
{
  @Consumption.semanticObject: 'MaintenanceOrder' 
  @ui.identification: [
    { type: #FOR_INTENT_BASED_NAVIGATION,
      semanticObjectAction: 'displayFactSheetCUSTOM',
      label: 'Overdue Maintenance Order' }]
  MaintenanceOrder;
}

 

now when clicking on the header of the card I go to F2175 instead of the standard F2173. 

So far everythings fine. But now I have a second Overview page for a different Team and they want the standard behaviour for the overdue orders. But since the metadata extension tells the card to use the semantic action "displayFactSheetCUSTOM" the same target definition is used and an error is displayed "Navigation to this application is not supported." since the target definition is in a different catalog.

How can I seperate this so the metadata extension only works for one specific app/card and not another?

entry.title: Re: Error related logs from Sybase
entry.content:
Find the current name and location of the errorlog:

 

select @@errorlog

 

Default location when ASE is initially built:

 

${SYBASE}/${SYBASE_ASE}/install/<ase_instance_name>.log

 

Default location when ASE is started will be defined in the runserver file, default name and location:

 

${SYBASE}/${SYBASE_ASE}/install/RUN_<ase_instance_name>

 

To change the location of the errorlog while ASE is up and running:

 

sp_errorlog 'change log', '/new/path/to/ASE/errorlog'

 

To change the location of the errorlog while ASE is shutdown - edit the runserver file to point to a new location.

entry.title: Consignment Process Using MRP Area
entry.content:
In this blog, I am sharing my experience with a requirement where the same material can be procured through both direct Procurement and Consignment Processes using the Planning Process.  The aim is to assist newcomers in grasping the diverse dynamics of the Consignment .  To achieve this, an MRP Area must execute the Planning Processes separately using different parameters. I will use the standard Procurement Process via Forecast Planning or Planned Independent Requirements (PIR), and the Consignment Process will be managed by the Reorder Point method. By default, the Material "Coal" is already available with an MRP Area at the plant level (type 01). A new MRP Area (type 02) will be required to meet this requirement.

What is MRP Area?

The MRP area represents an organizational unit for which material requirements Planning is carried out independently. The following are the three different types of MRP area:

  • Plant MRP Area.
  • MRP Areas for Storage Locations.
  • MRP Areas for Subcontractors.

Please note, MRP Area for Subcontractor is not in-scope of this blog.

Six Steps to complete this process:

  • Define the MRP Area (type 02) at storage location level.
  • Configure the material master with the newly created MRP Area.
  • Assign different parameters to each MRP Area.
  • View two MRP Areas for the same material with different parameters.
  • Generate Planned Independent Requirements (PIR).
  • Execute MRP using MD01N.

 1.   Define the MRP Area (type 02) for storage location level.

By default, system creates plant as an MRP Area.

Please note that “MRP Area” type 01 represents MRP Area at plant level that is already available by default as shown below.

Define MRP Area below plant level to meet the business requirement.

Please note that “MRP Area” type 02 represents MRP Area for Storage location level as shown below.

Assign storage location to MRP Area.

Please note that only storage location 5002 is assigned to MRP Area CONSIGN.

The below screen shows that material “COAL” is already extended at two different storage locations for the same plant as shown below.

2.   Configure the material master with the newly created MRP Area.

Configure material master “COAL” for MRP Area type 02.

As shown above, after MRP Area type 02 assignment for storage location in configuration, the “MRP area exits” check box is checked automatically. When click “MRP area” button, a newly created MRP Area “CONSIGN” is also shown above in the “Overview, Display MRP Area”.

3.   Assign different parameters to each MRP Area.

Parameters for MRP Area NJD1.

MRP1-View.

MRP2-View.

Parameters for MRP Area CONSIGN.

MRP1-View.

MRP2-View.

4.   View two MRP Areas for the same material with different parameters.

Material master “Coal” with MRP Area NJD1 is showing below.

Material master “Coal” with MRP Area CONSIGN is showing.

5.   Generate Planned Independent Requirements (PIR).

For the demo purpose, using  MD61, PIR is generated for MRP Area NJD1 as shown below.

Using MD04, generation of PIR can be confirmed as shown below.

Please note, for MRP Area CONSIGN, no PIR is required.

6.   Execute MRP using MD01N.

Using MD01N, Planning  is executed and as a result both Purchase Requisitions are generated.

Via PIR (Forecast).

Via Reorder Point.

                                          MRP Overview Flow Chart.

entry.title: Adoption Session - Creating & Editing in SAP Companion Recap
entry.content:
Link to Creating & Editing in SAP Companion session recording. 

Link to Creating & Editing in SAP Companion book covered during this session. 

Below are the Q&A topics that came up during the session:

  • If editing icons do not appear, is that in the SEN configuration setup?
    • Yes, the editing icon is something that you can activate in the SAP Companion configuration setup, it's a parameter. NOTE that Companion configuration is not the same section than SEN Server configuration.
  • What's the difference between Show Instantly and Show Bubble as a callout?
    • Show Instantly governs the text bubble and Show Bubble governs the entire element (icon + text) it's best just to try the different options. One setting changes the resulting options.
  • Can you share the specific steps needed to connect a cloud Workarea and edit the Manager Companion help content?
    • Check out the SAP Help portal and review the Companion Integration Guide.
    • Note: Any time you edit standard SAP content, it becomes your own custom content, and you are responsible for maintaining it!
    • Check out the blog: Getting Started with SAP Companion 
  • Do you have any resources for the Insert Placeholder option in the Text field interface?
    • Review the SAP Companion Authoring Guide and search for "Placeholder" to find the information.
  • When editing Companion content, what do the red and green traffic lights mean on the tours?
    • Red means the tour or content has not been published yet and the green light means it has been published. 
  • When the spotlight on hotspot occurs, does it prevent user from clicking elsewhere?
    • No, it does not.
  • To capture context for your Companion content, so they show up in the correct place, please ensure you have the correct Extensions downloaded. Review Extensions Overview | SAP Help Portal
  • If you have questions about integration Companion with third party applications, please talk to your VCSP.

@paolagomez @jairoramirez @robert_way @Blake_Hill

entry.title: bgRFC units in Transportation Management
entry.content:
Dear Reader,

The purpose of this blog is to provide general information about Background Remote Function Call (bgRFC) processing and monitoring in Transportation Management (TM).

Before we explore how TM utilizes the bgRFC framework, you may find it helpful to learn about bgRFC technology. For more information visit SAP Help Portal page bgRFC (Background Remote Function Call) .

This documentation is valid for the following SAP Releases:

  • SAP Transportation Management 9.5 and 9.6
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud 1709 and above

 

bgRFC units in Transportation Management

In TM bgRFC units (=units) are mainly used to execute triggers and strategies in the background without blocking the main processing flow.

  • Trigger execution:
    Triggers are created when a document cannot be updated due to a locking conflict, that is, when there is a foreign enqueue lock on the business document, or when a related document must be updated asynchronously. In these cases, the system creates bgRFC units to execute the corresponding BOBF action or ABAP class on the target document at a later point in time. For a description of the predefined triggers, see Customizing for TM under Basic Functions > General Settings > Define Triggers.
    Details about trigger concept are available on SAP Help Portal page Processing of Triggers.
  • Asynchronous Strategy execution:
    Asynchronous Create, Save and Change strategies that are set for a Document Type are also executed in bgRFC units.

bgRFC unit execution is always asynchronous and automatic, but an error or improper configuration can cause issues with that. Issues with bgRFC processing leads to inconsistent documents or an exploding number of units, which can cause a business down situation. Therefore, regular monitoring of bgRFC units is strongly recommended.

 

Mandatory bgRFC Configuration

Transaction: SBGRFCCONF

  • Inbound destination TM_BGRFC_INBOUND must exist. If this destination is not available, it must be created manually as described in Application Operations Guide under section “Trigger Processing”. Alternatively, you can use Report /SCMTMS/TRIGGER_SETUP_BGRFC to create the inbound destination. All bgRFC units created by TM application will be registered for this destination.

bgRFC Configuration - Inbound destination TM_BGRFC_INBOUND

  • Supervisor destination must be defined. You can find information about creating supervisor destination on SAP Help Portal page Creating a Supervisor Destination or in SAP Note 2309399 - How to use SBGRFCCONF effectively?.

bgRFC Configuration – Supervisor destination

For additional information on general bgRFC configuration, please refer to SAP Note 2309399-How to use SBGRFCCONF effectively?

 

bgRFC unit monitoring

There are two tools available to monitor bgRFC units:

  • bgRFC Monitor (= Monitor)
  • TM bgRFC Analysis Tool (= Analysis Tool).

bgRFC Monitor is the official tool for the administration of bgRFC units. You can display all bgRFC units, however it does not provide any application-specific information.

bgRFC Analysis Tool was developed by the TM application team to understand the application data behind a bgRFC unit. Therefore, Analysis Tool can be used to understand the content of bgRFC units in inbound destination TM_BGRFC_INBOUND and to have some additional statistical information regarding these units.

There can always be unexpected issues or application errors that are not easy to detect. Therefore, checking the bgRFC units from time to time will help you identify and resolve any issues in time.

A few examples which may arise in your system:

  • You suspect a missing or late update on a document.
    For example, if a freight unit is modified but the changes are not reflected on the related freight document.
  • You encounter permanent locking conflict for a document.
    This occurs when you are unable to edit a freight document for a longer time because it has been locked by another user.
  • You notice frequent PPF processing.
    In transaction SM13 there are high number of unprocessed or failed update requests.
  • You detect failed bgRFC units.
    If trigger or strategy execution fails due to an application error.

 

bgRFC Monitor

Transaction: SBGRFCMON

bgRFC Monitor displays the available bgRFC units in the system. It is used to check the availability of bgRFC units in general and to perform operations if necessary, such as a restart or deletion of the selected units.

On the Selection Screen, there is no need to fill out any of the selection options. If you want to display all units, you may need to increase the number of displayed units with parameter “Maximum No. Of Displayed Units”. TM bgRFC units are displayed under inbound destination TM_BGRFC_INBOUND.

bgRFC Monitor – bgRFC units for destination TM_BGRFC_INBOUND

 

bgRFC Unit Statuses

Based on the lifecycle of a bgRFC unit, you can observe the following statuses in the bgRFC Monitor:

  • Executable bgRFC unit: is executed automatically.
  • Locked bgRFC unit: is not executed until the lock is removed.
  • Failed bgRFC unit: was executed but failed.

Executable bgRFC units

bgRFC Monitor – Executable bgRFC unit

When a unit is created, it is displayed in the bgRFC Monitor with a green icon and executed automatically. After successful execution the unit is marked for deletion and will be deleted from the database tables later. Units that are marked for deletion are not displayed in the bgRFC Monitor.

Locked bgRFC units

Locked bgRFC units are displayed with a yellow icon in the bgRFC Monitor. You can identify the type of lock based on “Manual Lock” or “Debug Lock” columns.

  • Manual Lock

bgRFC Monitor – bgRFC unit with manual lock

In the monitor, you see that "Manual Lock" is checked. These units are delayed. This is a predefined delay which can be different for each unit. Once the specified delay expires for a unit, that unit is automatically “unlocked” and becomes executable.

  • Debug Lock

    bgRFC Monitor – bgRFC units with debug lock

In the monitor, you see that "Debug Lock" is checked. These units are permanently locked. A debug lock is set only if the process was debugged by a developer. The administrator must unlock them manually to have them executed.


Failed bgRFC units

bgRFC Monitor –Failed bgRFC unit

When a unit fails, it is displayed in the bgRFC Monitor with a red icon.
Failed units are never processed automatically. After resolving the error, the administrator must unlock them manually to have them executed again.

 
bgRFC Unit Lifecycle in Transportation Management

Once there is a need to register a trigger or a strategy, a new executable bgRFC unit is created. After successful execution the bgRFC unit is deleted. Technically, the bgRFC Unit is marked for deletion and only at a later point in time deleted permanently. You can specify the deletion time with parameter “Unit Delete Time (s)” in bgRFC Configuration.

During execution the system places an enqueue lock on the corresponding document.
In case the system cannot lock the document, the actual bgRFC unit is deleted and a new locked bgRFC unit is created. At this point, you will see the bgRFC unit with manual lock in the bgRFC Monitor. After, the predefined delay expires the unit is unlocked and executed.

There are two parameters set for the new locked unit:
- a retry counter which shows the number of the execution attempts
- a delay time which is determined based on the retry counter.

The retry counter is increased after each execution attempt. To avoid endless repetition, a maximum allowed number of retries is defined. When this number is reached, the actual bgRFC unit fails and no new unit is created. The maximum number of allowed retries is a TM specific parameter that is different from the parameter “Max. Auto. Retries” in bgRFC Configuration.

If any error occurs during execution the bgRFC unit fails.

 
Manual operations in bgRFC Monitor

As mentioned before, sometimes manual intervention is needed to unlock a bgRFC unit. This can be done for a single or for multiple units.

Single Operation

bgRFC Monitor – Delete unit lock for a single bgRFC unit

 

  1. Right click the selected bgRFC unit
  2. Choose “Delete Unit Lock”

Mass Operation

bgRFC Monitor – Delete unit lock for all selected bgRFC units

 

  1. Right click “Transactional Units”
  2. Choose “Mass Operations”
  3. Choose “Unblock Type T Units in Filter”

The parameter “Maximum No. of Displayed Units” is not considered during mass operation. All units that fulfils the selection criteria will be processed.

 

TM bgRFC Analysis Tool

Report: /SCMTMS/BGRFC_ANALYSE
Transaction: /SCMTMS/BGRFC

There might be cases when you need to know which business documents are processed by a bgRFC unit. If you are interested in such application information, you can use the TM bgRFC Analysis Tool.

TM bgRFC Analysis Tool – Selection Screen

The tool can display bgRFC units that are created under inbound destination TM_BGRFC_INBOUND.

The selection may take some time, especially, if you set a high number for “Max. No. of units”. In this case, you should execute the report as a background job and select option “Statistics, no details”.

 
Statistics in TM bgRFC Analysis Tool

TM bgRFC Analysis Tool – TM bgRFC Unit Statistics

After executing the report, you can find generic unit statistics on the right-hand side in section “TM bgRFC Unit Statistics”. These values are taken from the bgRFC Performance Monitor, which is also accessible through transaction SBGRFCPERFMON. The values are independent from the given selection criteria.

  • “Total” field shows the total number of available units. This includes all failed, locked, executable units, as well as units that are already marked for deletion.
  • “SYSFAIL State” shows the number of failed units.
  • “Executable” field shows the number of units that are waiting for execution or are being executed.

In the Totals, Top 10s and Statistics sections you can view the aggregated statistics based on different grouping criteria. These sections help you to detect if there is a specific trigger, strategy, or document that has an abnormal number of units.

TM bgRFC Analysis Tool – Statistics – Totals and Top 10

TM bgRFC Analysis Tool – Statistics – Statistics per Document Category

  • “Totals – Triggers” and “Totals – TOR Strategy” sections display how many business documents are registered for a certain trigger or strategy.
  • “Top 10 – Users” and “Top 10 – Documents” sections list the users or documents having the highest number of activities from bgRFC unit perspective.
  • “Trigger statistics – Per Document Category” and “TOR Strategy statistics – Per Document Category” sections show the triggers and strategies with the highest number of bgRFC units per document category.

Columns with suffix “(Example)” contains values taken from a random entry for that specific statistics group.

 
Unit details in TM bgRFC Analysis Tool

TM bgRFC Analysis Tool – Unit details – Overview

If you are interested in the application data behind a bgRFC unit, you can access this information by selecting either “Statistics with details” or “Only details in ALV” option on the selection screen.

Here, you see the retry counter, the state and the error messages for each unit, which provide information about the lifecycle status of a bgRFC unit:

  • Executable units are displayed without any State or Error Message.
  • Failed units have an error message in the Error Message column.
  • Locked units with manual lock have “* Delayed” value in the State column and a non-zero value in the Retry count column. If you select the “Display technical fields” option on the selection screen, the Delay Valid Until column shows the validity of the delay.

TM bgRFC Analysis Tool – Unit details – Delayed unit

  • Locked units with debug lock have a "Locked" value in the State column.

TM bgRFC Analysis Tool – Locked unit

  • Deleted bgRFC units have state “Deleted”. Once the bgRFC unit is permanently deleted, it will no longer be visible in the bgRFC Analysis Tool.

 

An example of trigger creation and monitoring:

Quantity is changed on a forwarding order while the assigned freight order is locked by a parallel process. In this case, the change cannot be propagated synchronously and the freight order can only be updated asynchronously by the TOR_UPDATE_FROM_RELATED_TOR trigger.

The executable unit is visible in the Analysis Tool. If the unit can be executed without any issues, you can see the unit with status Deleted.

In case of a locking conflict, this trigger cannot be executed. You see each execution attempt with an increasing Retry counter. For each execution attempt, there is a corresponding deleted bgRFC Unit.

The trigger is executed once the locking conflict is resolved. After successful execution, the forwarding order changes are visible on the freight order.

 

The Tool is delivered in the following SAP Notes:

  • For releases higher than or equal to S/4HANA 1809, use SAP Note 3343351 - bgRFC: Technical Correction, Analysis Tool 11.
  • For releases TM 9.5, TM 9.6, S/4HANA 1709, use SAP Note 3342574 - bgRFC: Analysis Tool with Checkpoint Group 1.

Global activation of Checkpoint Group /SCMTMS/BGRFC_REG is a prerequisite to catch the unit details. bgRFC units that are created while the Checkpoint Group is inactive will not be listed in the Analysis Tool.


Contribute to the content!

Thank you for reading my blog post. I hope it has helped you understand how bgRFC units are processed and how you can monitor them for Transportation Management. Feel free to explore the world of TM bgRFC units and post any processing or monitoring related question.

entry.title: Lead & Lag : SQL Window Functions and a bit more ...
entry.content:
Recently I was scrolling (not-mindlessly) videos on Youtube, when I stumbled upon an SQL coding problem. It wasn't anything super complicated, and I've seen similar problems on coding platforms like LeetCode before. But for some reason, this was the first time it had clicked with me. I was like, "Oh, I totally get this!" So I decided to give it a shot and write a blog post about it. And guess what? I realized that SQL window functions are actually pretty darn useful! 

In this blog, we will examine some of the SQL window functions available within ABAP-SQL and delve into their application through the use of examples.

So, here's the problem: We have a table called ZMOVIE_SEATS with two columns, SEAT_ID (Integer) and STATUS (Boolean).  SEAT_ID is an auto increment primary key (this column will have values like 1,2,3 … so on). Our goal is to find out which consecutive seat ids are occupied (or unoccupied which is simple flip in the solution). This problem is also known as the "Consecutive Available Seat Problem"  The trick is that we need to solve it using SQL alone. That's what caught my attention.  It's easy enough to solve using programming constructs like loops and conditionals, but doing it with SQL alone is what makes it interesting according to me.

Definitely, there could be multiple variations of the solutions, but the one I intend to present here is to utilize window functions, namely LEAD and LAG, while concurrently introducing the concept of window functions to readers who may not yet be acquainted with it.

Sample Input Table Data:

Expected Output: 3, 4, 5

Before we get to solving it using window functions, lets learn what are the '"window functions"

What are window functions?

Window functions in SQL are a powerful tool for performing calculations across a set of rows that are related to the current row. They provide the ability to perform aggregate-like operations (e.g., SUM, COUNT, AVG) or ranking calculations (e.g., RANK, ROW_NUMBER) without actually grouping the result set into fewer rows. This means that each row maintains its individual identity while still incorporating information from its surrounding "window" of rows.

Now, let's look at definitions of some key terms and concepts:

  • Windows: A window is essentially a subset of rows within your result set. You define the window using the OVER clause. It can encompass the entire result set, a partition (group) of rows, or a specific range of rows relative to the current row.
  • Window Frame: The window frame is the specific set of rows within the window that are used in the calculation for a particular row. You can control the frame's boundaries using keywords like ROWS BETWEEN, UNBOUNDED PRECEDING, and CURRENT ROW.
  • Partitioning (PARTITION BY): This clause divides the result set into distinct groups or partitions. Window functions are then applied separately within each partition.
  • Ordering (ORDER BY): This clause sorts the rows within the window or partition, which is crucial for calculations like running totals or rankings.

Types of Window Functions

  • Aggregate Window Functions: These functions (SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX) work similarly to regular aggregate functions but without collapsing rows.
  • Ranking Window Functions: These functions (RANK, DENSE_RANK, ROW_NUMBER, NTILE) assign rankings or numerical positions to rows within their window.
  • Value Window Functions: These functions (LAG, LEAD, FIRST_VALUE, LAST_VALUE) access values from other rows within the window.

Why Use Window Functions?

  • Flexibility: You can perform complex calculations without restructuring your result set.
  • Performance: They are often more efficient than solutions involving self-joins or subqueries.
  • Expressiveness: They make your SQL queries more concise and readable.

Sample solution for the Consecutive Available Seat Problem:

 

TYPES: BEGIN OF movie_seat,
         seat_id TYPE i,
         free    TYPE abap_bool,
       END OF movie_seat,
       movie_seats TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF movie_seat WITH NON-UNIQUE KEY seat_id.

DATA(lt_movie_seats) = VALUE movie_seats( ( seat_id = 1 free = abap_true )
                                          ( seat_id = 2 free = abap_false )
                                          ( seat_id = 3 free = abap_true )
                                          ( seat_id = 4 free = abap_true )
                                          ( seat_id = 5 free = abap_true ) ).

SELECT seat_id                               AS seat_id,
       free                                  AS current_free,
       LAG( free ) OVER( ORDER BY seat_id )  AS previous_free,
       LEAD( free ) OVER( ORDER BY seat_id ) AS next_free
  FROM _movie_seats AS movie_seats
  ORDER BY seat_id
  INTO TABLE (lt_tab).

SELECT seat_id FROM _tab AS data1
  WHERE current_free = 
    AND ( previous_free =  OR next_free =  )
  INTO TABLE (lt_output).

cl_demo_output=>display_data( value = lt_output ).

 

Program Output:

Now let's explore what do these functions LEAD and LAG do?

The window functions LEAD and LAG are designed to give you access to data from rows other than the current row you're processing within a result set. They operate within a specified window (a subset of rows) and help you analyze data in relation to its neighboring rows.

  • LEAD: Peels back the curtain to show you data from rows that come after the current row in your window. It's like having a sneak peek into the future.
  • LAG: Takes you back in time to reveal data from rows that precede the current row. Think of it as looking through a rearview mirror.

These have the following syntax:

LEAD|LAG( sql_exp1[, diff[, sql_exp2]]

diff determines the number of rows to either look forward or backward for LEAD and LAG respectively. If diff is not specified a value of 1 is assumed implicitly.If the row determined by diff is not in the current window, the result is the null value by default.

OVER Clause is essential for both LEAD and LAG. It defines the window (set of rows) within which the functions operate. The ORDER BY clause within OVER is crucial to establish the sequence of rows.

The below illustrations should be helpful in understanding the concept:

LAG:

LEAD:

Let's look at two more window functions ...

Since we are on the topic of window functions, lets also explore two more window functions namely FIRST_VALUE and LAST_VALUE with an example:

Imagine we have a logging table with below entries:

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Now, if we are tasked to identify the first and the last log entry for every user per day then we achieve it by using the above introduced window functions as shown below:

 

TYPES: BEGIN OF lty_entry_log,
         entry_id TYPE char5,
         time     TYPE tims,
         date     TYPE date,
         user_id  TYPE char10,
       END OF lty_entry_log,
       ltt_entry_log TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF lty_entry_log WITH NON-UNIQUE KEY entry_id.

DATA lt_entry_log TYPE ltt_entry_log.

lt_entry_log = VALUE #( ( entry_id = '10001' time = '084534' date = '20231010' user_id = 'mark' )
                        ( entry_id = '10002' time = '093012' date = '20231010' user_id = 'mark' )
                        ( entry_id = '10003' time = '111547' date = '20231010' user_id = 'john' )
                        ( entry_id = '10004' time = '123022' date = '20231010' user_id = 'john' )
                        ( entry_id = '10005' time = '142551' date = '20231010' user_id = 'mark' )
                        ( entry_id = '10006' time = '083609' date = '20231011' user_id = 'john' )
                        ( entry_id = '10007' time = '092841' date = '20231011' user_id = 'mark' )
                        ( entry_id = '10008' time = '113012' date = '20231011' user_id = 'mark' )
                        ( entry_id = '10009' time = '121855' date = '20231011' user_id = 'mark' )
                        ( entry_id = '10010' time = '143639' date = '20231011' user_id = 'john' ) ).

SELECT DISTINCT user_id,
                date,
                FIRST_VALUE( time ) OVER( PARTITION  BY user_id, date ORDER BY date ) AS first_entry,
                LAST_VALUE( time ) OVER( PARTITION BY user_id, date  ORDER BY date )  AS last_entry
  FROM _entry_log AS log_entry
  ORDER BY date
  INTO TABLE (lt_result).

cl_demo_output=>display_data( lt_result ).

 

Upon, execution we get the following output which is as expected:

 

 

 

 

So what do FIRST_VALUE and LAST_VALUE essentially do?

Consider a window, which is a subset of rows from a query result. These functions offer a glimpse into values within that window:

  • FIRST_VALUE: retrieves the value from the very first row in the window.
  • LAST_VALUE: obtains the value from the very last row in the window.

The window is specified using an OVER clause. Similar to LEAD and LAG, FIRST_VALUE and LAST_VALUE require this clause. It's crucial to specify the ordering of the rows within the window using ORDER BY to determine the "first" or "last" row.

OVER Clause: The OVER clause defines the window. It shares similarities with LEAD and LAG. ORDER BY within the OVER clause is essential in determining which row is considered the "first" or "last".

PARTITION BY: While optional, PARTITION BY is useful for dividing data into groups.

Conclusion

Window functions offer a lot more flexibility to do code push down and learning and understanding them will definitely help a developer write efficient SQL!

Thanks for reading! Hope you found the topics covered in this blog interesting and worth exploring further.

To read more about the window functions available in ABAP SQL then you can explore the standard SAP documentation using the link: https://help.sap.com/doc/abapdocu_latest_index_htm/latest/en-US/index.htm?file=abapselect_over.htm

You can also look at sample code within the following ABAP classes to learn more:

  • CL_DEMO_SELECT_OVER_LEAD_LAG
  • CL_DEMO_SELECT_FIRST_LAST

And .. in case you have alternative solutions to the problems discussed in this blog, do share them in the comment sections, it would be really cool to know different techniques that can be applied!
